items.prompt_md: In the opposite-ends palindrome check, what condition ends the loop **successfully** (no mismatch ever found)?
options: The string has been reversed and compared
items.explanation_md: The pointers walk inward one pair at a time; once `lo >= hi` there are no pairs left to check, so every pair matched and the sequence is a palindrome.

items.prompt_md: You need to test a symmetric property across an array or string — the pair at each pair of matching positions must satisfy some condition — using O(1) extra space. Which approach fits?
options: Reverse the sequence into a new copy and compare element-by-element
Sort the sequence first, then compare adjacent elements
Start pointers at both ends and walk them inward, checking the condition at each pair
Use a hash set to record every element seen so far
items.answer: Start pointers at both ends and walk them inward, checking the condition at each pair
items.explanation_md: Symmetric, whole-sequence condition + O(1) space is the fingerprint for opposite-ends two pointers. Reversing costs O(n) extra space; sorting or hashing throws away the positional structure the check needs.

items.prompt_md: What is the time and extra-space complexity of the opposite-ends two-pointer palindrome check (ignoring the input string itself)?
options: O(n log n) time, O(1) extra space
items.explanation_md: Each pointer moves inward at most n/2 times and never backtracks, so the pass is O(n) time; only the two index variables are extra memory, so it's O(1) extra space.
